does: This paper investigates jailbreak vulnerabilities in large language models across multiple languages, revealing that safety mechanisms designed primarily for English are significantly weaker for other languages. The authors identify and analyze two distinct failure modes: unintentional jailbreaks where users query LLMs in non-English languages and inadvertently bypass safety filters, and intentional jailbreaks where adversaries deliberately combine multilingual prompts with malicious instructions. Through systematic experiments, they demonstrate that unsafe content generation increases substantially with language scarcity, with low-resource languages showing approximately three times higher rates of harmful outputs compared to high-resource languages. This empirical finding quantifies a critical asymmetry in safety alignment across the multilingual landscape.
connects: This work builds on established research into LLM safety and jailbreak vulnerabilities, extending beyond the English-centric evaluation paradigm that has dominated the field. It responds to earlier work on prompt injection attacks and adversarial inputs to language models by demonstrating that existing safeguards lack robustness when generalized to multilingual settings. The paper connects to broader concerns about equitable AI safety, relating to recent findings that safety training concentrates linguistic bias and to research showing that non-English languages receive less alignment attention. It also draws on multilingual NLP literature showing performance degradation in low-resource settings, applying this insight to the safety domain rather than conventional task performance.
enables: This work opens critical directions for more inclusive and robust AI safety practices. It establishes the need for multilingual safety evaluation as a standard requirement in LLM development and testing, moving beyond English-only benchmarks. The findings enable future research into cross-lingual transfer of adversarial attacks, defensive techniques that generalize across languages, and multilingual alignment methods that do not sacrifice safety for linguistic diversity. It motivates development of safety mechanisms that account for language resource disparities and points toward broader implications for global deployment of LLMs in non-English contexts, particularly in lower-resource languages where communities may face disproportionate risks from insufficiently aligned systems.
